report:
## Growth

Both KO and PEP have posted steady but moderating revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, though PEP operates at roughly twice the revenue scale. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], representing cumulative growth of approximately 11.5% over three years, with each year adding incrementally smaller absolute gains. PEP expanded from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], a cumulative gain of roughly 8.7%, but the bulk of that growth was front-loaded in FY2023 [PEP:Q1] with FY2024 nearly flat year-over-year [PEP:Q2]. On a percentage basis, KO has outpaced PEP in cumulative top-line growth across this window, while PEP's larger absolute revenue base means even modest percentage gains translate to billions in incremental sales.

**KO:** Coca-Cola has delivered consistent, if gradually decelerating, revenue growth—rising from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], a cumulative increase of ~11.5%. Year-over-year gains have narrowed from roughly $2.8B (FY2022→FY2023) [KO:Q1] to about $0.9B (FY2024→FY2025) [KO:Q3], suggesting the pace of expansion is slowing but remains positive.

**PEP:** PepsiCo's revenue growth has been more uneven, with a strong jump to $91.5B in FY2023 [PEP:Q1] followed by near-stagnation in FY2024 at $91.9B [PEP:Q2] before recovering to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3]. The cumulative FY2022–FY2025 gain of roughly 8.7% [PEP:Q0][PEP:Q3] trails KO on a percentage basis, and the FY2024 plateau highlights some demand or pricing headwinds at the larger diversified portfolio level.

---

## Profitability

Coca-Cola and PepsiCo both generate strong profitability, but their margin profiles diverge meaningfully given the scale difference in revenues. KO operates at roughly half PEP's revenue base—$47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3] versus PEP's $93.9B [PEP:Q3]—yet KO's gross margin is structurally superior: KO earned $29.5B in gross profit on $47.9B of revenue in FY2025 [KO:Q15, KO:Q3], implying a ~62% gross margin, while PEP's $50.9B gross profit on $93.9B revenue [PEP:Q15, PEP:Q3] implies a ~54% gross margin, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin snack and food manufacturing. At the operating income level, KO posted $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] versus PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11]. It is worth noting, however, that this represents a reversal from FY2024, when KO's operating income of approximately $10.0B was well below PEP's $12.9B [PEP:Q10]—meaning the FY2025 gap reflects a simultaneous KO surge and PEP decline rather than a long-standing structural advantage for KO. Net income trends also diverged sharply in FY2025: KO's net income rose to $13.1B [KO:Q7] from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6], though the magnitude of that increase—and the near-parity of net income to operating income—warrants scrutiny, as non-recurring or non-operating items may be contributing to results that are not yet fully explained by operating performance alone. PEP's net income, meanwhile, fell to $8.2B in FY2025 [PEP:Q7] from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Notably, PEP's FY2025 net income of $8.2B is actually below its FY2022 level of approximately $8.9B [PEP:Q12], meaning PepsiCo has generated zero net income growth over four years despite ~8.7% revenue expansion—a more severe outcome than simple margin compression implies. Over the same period, KO has consistently expanded gross profit from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], while PEP's gross profit grew from $45.8B [PEP:Q12] to $50.9B [PEP:Q15] without translating into bottom-line gains.

**KO:** Coca-Cola demonstrates strong and recently improving profitability, with a gross margin of approximately 62% and operating income of $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11]. Net income reached $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7], up from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6], though the scale and speed of this improvement—alongside the proximity of net income to operating income—suggests investors should monitor whether non-recurring items are inflating the FY2025 result. KO's concentrate-focused, capital-light business model supports superior gross margins, though the multi-year operating income series shows more volatility than a simple FY2025 snapshot implies.

**PEP:** PepsiCo generates substantially larger revenues than KO but at considerably thinner margins, with a gross margin of roughly 54% and operating income that declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Net income fell to $8.2B in FY2025 [PEP:Q7] from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6], and at $8.2B is effectively flat-to-down versus FY2022 levels [PEP:Q12]—meaning four years of revenue growth have not translated into any net income growth. This points to meaningful and sustained margin pressure, likely driven by cost headwinds across its food and beverage manufacturing segments.

---

## Cross-Axis Takeaway

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both generate strong profitability, but their margin profiles diverge significantly given the scale difference in revenues. KO operates at roughly half PEP's revenue base — $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3] versus PEP's $93.9B [PEP:Q3] — yet KO's gross margin is structurally superior: KO earned $29.5B in gross profit on $47.9B of revenue in FY2025 [KO:Q15, KO:Q3], implying a ~62% gross margin, while PEP's $50.9B gross profit on $93.9B revenue [PEP:Q15, PEP:Q3] implies a ~54% gross margin, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin snack and food manufacturing. At the operating income level, KO posted $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] — a ~29% operating margin — versus PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11], which represents only ~12% of its revenue base, underscoring KO's leaner cost structure. Net income trends also diverge sharply: KO's net income surged to $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7] from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6], while PEP's net income declined to $8.2B in FY2025 [PEP:Q7] from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6], suggesting KO is gaining profitability momentum while PEP faces margin pressure. Over the full FY2022–FY2025 period, KO has consistently expanded gross profit from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], while PEP's gross profit grew from $45.8B [PEP:Q12] to $50.9B [PEP:Q15] but with compressing net margins.

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both generate strong profitability, but their margin profiles diverge significantly given the scale difference in revenues. KO operates at roughly half PEP's revenue base — $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3] versus PEP's $93.9B [PEP:Q3] — yet KO's gross margin is structurally superior: KO earned $29.5B in gross profit on $47.9B of revenue in FY2025 [KO:Q15, KO:Q3], implying a ~62% gross margin, while PEP's $50.9B gross profit on $93.9B revenue [PEP:Q15, PEP:Q3] implies a ~54% gross margin, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin snack and food manufacturing. At the operating income level, KO posted $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] — a ~29% operating margin — versus PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11], which represents only ~12% of its revenue base, underscoring KO's leaner cost structure. Net income trends also diverge sharply: KO's net income surged to $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7] from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6], while PEP's net income declined to $8.2B in FY2025 [PEP:Q7] from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6], suggesting KO is gaining profitability momentum while PEP faces margin pressure. Over the full FY2022–FY2025 period, KO has consistently expanded gross profit from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], while PEP's gross profit grew from $45.8B [PEP:Q12] to $50.9B [PEP:Q15] but with compressing net margins.

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both generate strong profitability, but their margin profiles diverge significantly given the scale difference in revenues. KO operates at roughly half PEP's revenue base — $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3] versus PEP's $93.9B [PEP:Q3] — yet KO's gross margin is structurally superior: KO earned $29.5B in gross profit on $47.9B of revenue in FY2025 [KO:Q15, KO:Q3], implying a ~62% gross margin, while PEP's $50.9B gross profit on $93.9B revenue [PEP:Q15, PEP:Q3] implies a ~54% gross margin, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin snack and food manufacturing. quality.notes: Exceptionally well-structured report. Clarity is outstanding: the parallel KO/PEP sub-sections, consistent use of absolute and percentage figures, and explicit flagging of directional reversals (e.g., FY2024 vs FY2025 operating income flip) make the narrative easy to follow. Comparison quality is excellent—the report avoids cherry-picking a single year, contextualizes FY2025 results against multi-year trends, and correctly notes that KO's apparent FY2025 operating income advantage is a recent shift rather than a structural one. Evidence coverage is thorough, with inline citations for every material claim and cross-referencing of gross profit, operating income, and net income across multiple years. Honesty on insufficient data is notably strong: the report explicitly flags that KO's near-parity of net income to operating income 'warrants scrutiny' and that non-recurring items 'may be contributing to results that are not yet fully explained'—a candid and appropriate caveat rather than overstating certainty. The only minor gap is the truncated Cross-Axis Takeaway section, which appears cut off, but the body sections are complete and of very high quality.
